title: QUIVER: Quantum-Informed Views for Enhanced Representations in Large ML Models
authors: Aritra Bal, Michael Binder, Markus Klute, Benedikt Maier, Michael Spannowsky
abstract: Large machine learning models benefit substantially from multimodal inputs that provide a complementary view of the same example. We introduce QUIVER (QUantum-Informed Views for Enhanced Representations, a paradigm that enriches classical data-driven features with a quantum Fisher view: a geometrically motivated, basis-independent summary of higher-order correlations captured by a variational quantum circuit (VQC) trained to perform the same task. Unlike classical feature augmentation, the quantum Fisher information matrix encodes the intrinsic geometry of the learned quantum state manifold. While this feature map, motivated by quantum information theory, is ordinarily non-trivial to model classically, it can surface statistical structure that additional classical data or model capacity finds difficult to learn. This makes the quantum Fisher view a genuinely complementary modality rather than a redundant one. We demonstrate that QUIVER improves standard performance metrics on two benchmark datasets from very different fields: QM9 for predicting molecule properties, and JetClass for predicting jet flavor at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The core contribution, however, is domain-agnostic: the quantum Fisher view can be fused into a broad class of model architectures via targeted modifications to the base architecture, to incorporate information about the quantum geometry of the problem. These results demonstrate that quantum-geometric features, extracted from simulated variational circuits, can deliver measurable value for standard machine learning tasks, well before the advent of fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
summary: This paper introduces QUIVER, a method that enriches classical machine learning features by incorporating a 'quantum Fisher view' derived from variational quantum circuits. It demonstrates that this quantum-geometric information provides a genuinely complementary signal, leading to measurable performance gains in diverse fields like quantum chemistry and particle physics. The work shows that quantum insights can boost ML models even without fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
main_problem: The paper addresses how to enhance the representational power of large machine learning models by integrating information derived from quantum mechanics.
main_result: The proposed QUIVER framework successfully improves performance on both molecular property prediction (QM9) and high-energy physics jet classification tasks.
method: It introduces the quantum Fisher view, derived from a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC), to augment classical feature vectors, treating this quantum-geometric information as a complementary modality.
model_or_system: The methods are applied to two distinct domains: predicting molecular properties (QM9) and classifying particle jets at the LHC. The core mathematical tool is the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), which characterizes the geometry of the quantum state manifold.
key_observables: For jet tagging, the Area Under the Curve (AUC) score and QCD background rejection rate are key; for molecular regression, the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of the HOMO-LUMO gap is used.
important_parameters: The study uses restricted qubit counts (e.g., up to 10 qubits) for encoding, and the performance is benchmarked across varying dataset sizes (e.g., 0.1M to 5M jets).
main_assumptions: The quantum view is assumed to provide genuinely complementary information that classical feature augmentation alone cannot capture, and the QFIM calculation is tractable on classical simulators.
figures_summary: Figures compare the performance (e.g., MAE curves for molecular regression) of the QUIVER-augmented models against their classical baselines, showing consistent improvements.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the need for multimodal inputs, introduces QUIVER by defining the quantum Fisher view via VQCs, and then demonstrates its efficacy by applying the framework to two distinct benchmarks: QM9 and LHC jet tagging.
why_interesting: While the application is ML-focused, the reliance on the QFIM—a fundamental measure of quantum distinguishability—provides a novel, information-theoretic lens for feature engineering that connects quantum geometry to classical data science.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Validation MAE during training for the DimeNet++ baseline and the quantum-inspired QDimeNet++ model. Solid lines show the mean across N = 10 paired seeds (identical data splits and initialization RNG state across the two models; only the architecture differs), smoothed with a 3-epoch rolling window. Shaded bands denote ±1 sample standard deviation across seeds (calculated using standard library functions with ddof=1). The lower panel shows the per-epoch paired difference ∆MAE = MAEDimeNet++ −MAEQDimeNet++ with the correspond- ing ±1σ band over the same paired seeds; positive values indicate lower MAE for QDimeNet++.
